mnemonics.title: Code Generation Issues - SIRCCE
mnemonics.content: SIRCCE covers the main issues: Selection (instruction), Allocation (register), Register assignment, Calling conventions, Code motion (optimization), Evaluation order. Think: 'Sir, Choose Code Execution Order' - an awkward but memorable phrase.

mnemonics.title: Register Allocation Phases
mnemonics.content: Think 'Alloc Then Assign': First Allocation (decide WHAT goes in registers), then Assignment (decide WHICH physical register). Allocation is the coarse decision, assignment is the fine detail.

mnemonics.title: Live Variable Definition
mnemonics.content: LIVE = Last use Before Next Definition. A variable is live if its value will be read again before being overwritten. Remember: 'L-I-V-E = Last Instruction Value Expected' - the value is Expected to be used later.

acronyms.usage: The internal code format (3-address code, DAG, etc.) between parsing and code generation.
Graph representation of a program showing all paths that might be traversed during execution, used for code generation and optimization.
Intermediate representation that captures computation structure while identifying common subexpressions and reusing nodes.
IR form where each variable is assigned exactly once, simplifying many optimizations and register allocation.
Three types of data hazards in instruction scheduling that must be considered during code generation.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a social network where people who 'talk' (are simultaneously live) cannot share the same room (register). If A and B are both live at the same time, draw an edge between them. Then try to color the graph with minimum colors (registers) such that adjacent nodes have different colors.

Think of a variable's lifetime as a timeline from birth (first assignment) to death (last use before next assignment). Variables whose timelines overlap cannot share a register. Draw horizontal bars for each variable showing their live ranges - overlapping bars = interference = cannot share register.

Imagine looking through a small 'peephole' at a sequence of generated instructions. You can only see 3-5 instructions at a time. Scan across the code looking for obvious improvements: redundant loads, useless stores, branch to next instruction, etc.

analogies.analogy: Think of registers as parking spaces and variables as cars. If two cars (variables) are never on the road (live) at the same time, they can share the same parking space. When all spaces are full and another car needs to park, one must leave (spill to memory) temporarily.

Like translating a book from one language to another. There may be multiple ways to express the same idea. A good translator (instruction selector) chooses the most natural and efficient phrasing (instruction sequence) for the target language (machine).

Code generation is like a factory's final assembly line. Intermediate code is the parts, and code generation is assembling them into the final product (machine code). Just as assembly must consider available tools (registers), assembly order, and efficiency, code generation must handle similar constraints.
